preFix.title: Joining a community energy co-operative
preFix.subtitle: How community solar and wind shares work, what returns to expect, and how to find schemes near you
preFix.excerpt: A community energy co-operative raises money from local shareholders to fund a solar or wind installation. Shareholders receive a dividend and can buy electricity from the scheme at preferential rates. This explains the legal structure, typical financial returns, risk profile, and how to find a scheme accepting investment near you.
preFix.sourceNotes: Community Energy England and Community Energy Scotland registers of community schemes. Co-operatives UK guidance on community benefit societies and industrial and provident societies. Ofgem SEG tariff register (open access). DECC 'Community Energy: call to action' (2014) for legal structure descriptions.

content.text: Community energy co-operatives fund solar, wind, or hydro through shares sold to local people. The co-operative owns the asset, earns revenue, and pays dividends to shareholders. The
community benefit society
is the most common legal form in England, Wales, and Scotland.
What a share investment looks like
Minimum share investments typically run from 250 to 1,000 pounds. Maximum holdings are capped (often at 20,000 to 100,000 pounds) to prevent any one investor dominating the co-operative. Shares are
withdrawable

: the society can repay them at face value after a minimum holding period (usually 3 to 5 years), but they are not tradeable on a secondary market. If the society fails, shares are not covered by the FSCS deposit protection scheme.
Typical financial returns

Most community energy co-operatives target dividends of 3 to 6% per year on the face value of shares. Actual dividends depend on the generation revenue, the society's debt service obligations (if any), and running costs. A 500-pound share at 4% dividend yields 20 pounds per year. The
community energy fund
typically distributes 0.5 to 1 pound per MWh generated to local community projects.
Risk profile

Main risks: lower generation than forecast, lower electricity prices with no Power Purchase Agreement, and management problems. A 2022 survey found UK community energy schemes had a default rate under 3% since 2010.
Finding a scheme
Community Energy England has a searchable map of schemes. Community Energy Scotland and Wales have equivalent registers. New share offers appear on Ethex and the Triodos investment platform.
Questions to ask before investing
Has the scheme had an independent energy yield assessment? Does it match actual generation to date?
Does the society have a Power Purchase Agreement that fixes electricity sale price, or does it sell at market rate?
What is the withdrawal notice period and has the society repaid shares on request before?
What is the remaining term on the site lease or grid connection agreement?
